name: Log Analyzer
scenario: You're building an internal tool for your DevOps team. Every backend service produces logs — text files with millions of lines containing timestamps, log levels, and messages. When something breaks at 3 AM, engineers need to quickly find: how many errors happened, which endpoint had the most issues, what time range was affected. Your tool parses these logs and produces a summary report. This is real production tooling — companies like Splunk, Datadog, Sumo Logic build entire products around this. You're building a simplified version.
description: Build a `LogAnalyzer` class that processes a string containing multiple log lines and produces a formatted summary report. Each log line has format: `[TIMESTAMP] [LEVEL] [ENDPOINT] message`. Practice: string parsing (split), text processing (StringBuilder), formatted output (String.format), and converting between strings and numbers.

core.description: `parseLog(String logs)` splits the input by newline into individual log lines
`countByLevel(String[] lines, String level)` counts how many lines contain the given level (INFO, WARN, ERROR, DEBUG). Uses .contains.
`getMostActiveEndpoint(String[] lines)` finds the endpoint mentioned most often. Returns its name.
`generateReport(String logs)` produces a formatted summary report using StringBuilder
Report includes: total lines, count of each level (INFO, WARN, ERROR), most active endpoint
Uses String.format for at least one aligned column in the report

bonus.description: Add `extractErrors(String[] lines)` returning only the ERROR lines as a String[] for further analysis
Add `getErrorRate(String[] lines)` returning percentage of ERROR lines (e.g., '12.5%')
Parse the timestamp from each line and find time range (earliest, latest)
Print error counts BY ENDPOINT — which endpoint had the most errors

data_to_use:
Use this hardcoded multi-line log string:

```java
String logs = 
    "[09:01:23] [INFO] [/api/users] Request received\n" +
    "[09:01:24] [INFO] [/api/users] Response sent 200\n" +
    "[09:02:15] [WARN] [/api/payments] Slow query detected\n" +
    "[09:03:45] [ERROR] [/api/payments] Database timeout\n" +
    "[09:04:12] [INFO] [/api/users] Request received\n" +
    "[09:04:13] [ERROR] [/api/payments] Connection refused\n" +
    "[09:05:01] [INFO] [/api/orders] Request received\n" +
    "[09:05:30] [ERROR] [/api/payments] Database timeout\n" +
    "[09:06:15] [WARN] [/api/orders] Retry triggered\n" +
    "[09:07:00] [INFO] [/api/users] Response sent 200";
```

Expected counts:
- Total lines: 10
- INFO: 5
- WARN: 2
- ERROR: 3
- Most active endpoint: /api/users (4 mentions) — though /api/payments also has 4. Either is acceptable based on tie-breaking strategy.

Actually let me count more carefully:
- /api/users: 4 (lines 1, 2, 5, 7, 10) — that's 5
- /api/payments: 4 (lines 3, 4, 6, 8)
- /api/orders: 2 (lines 7, 9)

Most active: /api/users with 5 mentions.
ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. The trickiest method is getMostActiveEndpoint — you need to extract endpoint substrings from each line and count their frequencies. Without HashMap (Collections module), you'll use parallel arrays or nested loops.
submission_instructions: Save as LogAnalyzer.java. Run it. Test with different log inputs — change levels, add lines, see report adapt. Push to GitHub — this is real DevOps tooling. Add to your portfolio with note 'string processing + report generation' as the highlight.
tips_for_success: Split by "\n" to get array of log lines
For countByLevel: simple — loop, check if line.contains("[" + level + "]"), count.
For endpoint extraction: each line has [/api/...] pattern. Find indexOf('[/'), then next ']' after.
For most-active endpoint without HashMap: collect all endpoints in a String[], then for each unique one, count occurrences.
Pre-OOP approach: parallel arrays for endpoint names and counts.
Use String.format with %-10s and %4d for the level table alignment.
Build the entire report in a single StringBuilder — don't print line by line.
concepts_reinforced: indexOf and substring for endpoint extraction
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Beyond the syntax, you experience LOG PROCESSING — the foundation of observability in modern software. Every major company runs log analysis on TB of logs daily. The patterns you're using (parse → extract → count → report) are universal. With small tweaks, your code could process Apache server logs, application crash reports, transaction histories. The architecture is the architecture used by Splunk, ELK Stack, Sumo Logic. You're now thinking like an SRE.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add severity scoring: ERROR = 10, WARN = 3, INFO = 1. Calculate total severity score.
Detect patterns: 'database timeout' appears 2x in errors. Flag repeat errors.
Compute statistics: avg errors per minute (parse timestamps)
Output as JSON instead of plain text
Filter by time range (only count errors in last N minutes)
Convert to use HashMap (preview of Collections module — endpoint counts become a HashMap)
